title: Lamb Shift of Landau Levels in Two-Dimensional Electron Systems in a Multimode Resonator
authors: Aleksandr Shabanov, Georgy Alymov, Dmitry Svintsov
abstract: The use of resonators to modify the behavior of electromagnetic systems demonstrates its potential for application in a wide range of problems. However, existing theoretical studies often resort to the single-mode approximation, rarely considering a second resonator mode. In this paper, we show that including a large number of resonator modes in the model significantly enhances the softening effect of the cyclotron frequency of a two-dimensional electron system. We address this problem by demonstrating the possibility of reducing the system to a set of coupled harmonic oscillators and finding the eigenfrequencies of the oscillators. This is made possible by applying the self-energy method for modes in one polarization and the method for finding the eigenvalues of matrices that have undergone first-rank updating for modes in the perpendicular polarization.
summary: This paper moves beyond the single-mode approximation to show that a large number of resonator modes significantly enhances the frequency reduction (softening) of cyclotron motion in a 2D electron system. By treating the system as coupled harmonic oscillators, the authors demonstrate that high mode density can drive the system's eigenfrequency toward zero. This provides new insights into the physics of light-matter interaction in the strong-coupling regime.
main_problem: Investigating the Lamb shift of Landau levels in a two-dimensional electron system (2DES) when coupled to a multimode resonator, specifically addressing how multiple resonator modes affect the softening of the cyclotron frequency.
main_result: The study demonstrates that including a large number of resonator modes significantly enhances the softening effect of the cyclotron frequency, potentially allowing the lowest eigenfrequency to approach zero.
method: The authors reduce the Hamiltonian to a set of coupled harmonic oscillators and solve for the eigenfrequencies using the self-energy method for x-polarized modes and a first-rank update method for y-polarized modes.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional electron system (2DES) in a perpendicular magnetic field placed within a multimode resonator, modeled by a Hamiltonian including electron kinetic energy and resonator photon modes.
key_observables: Lowest eigenfrequency (omega_min), cyclotron frequency (omega_c), and the position of the lower polariton level.
important_parameters: Coupling constant (chi_xy), mode density, photon mass, resonator volume, and the regime of strong photon interaction/high mode density.
main_assumptions: The 2DES is much smaller than the resonator wavelength (dipole approximation), the 2DES is located at a high-symmetry point, and the system is treated within the dipole approximation regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the 2DES in a resonator; Figure 2 plots the lowest eigenfrequency versus the number of photon modes comparing numerical and analytical results; Figure 3 shows the lower polariton level position versus the lowest resonator frequency for different volumes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of multi-mode effects, defines the Hamiltonian and physical model, details the mathematical reduction to harmonic oscillators using self-energy and first-rank update methods, presents numerical and analytical results regarding frequency softening, and discusses scaling laws and geometric implications.
why_interesting: It explores the transition from single-mode to multi-mode cavity QED, specifically looking at the 'deep-strong' coupling regime and the emergence of collective phenomena like polariton softening, which is highly relevant to cavity-mediated light-matter interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic diagram of the system under study. The solution took into account modes polarized along Lx and Ly due to the location of the two-dimensional electrical system at the resonator point, where all modes are polarized along one of these directions. The two-dimensional electron system is small compared to the wavelength of the resonator modes (a << λres), which leads to a uniform ﬁeld of virtual photons

FIG. 2. Plots of level positions versus the number of photons for various distances between the resonator mode eigenfre- quencies. Numerical solution (dots) and approximate analyt- ical solution (30) (solid lines)

FIG. 3. Position of the lower polariton level as a function of the frequency of the lowest photon mode, calculated numer- ically (dots) and using the approximate analytical formula (30) (solid lines) for various resonator volumes.
